verdicts.note: No documented judicial-philosophy evidence places him on either pole. His stated approach is boilerplate that both poles claim ('rule of law', 'fair, independent, accessible', 'faithfully applying Washington's Constitution and laws', analyzing 'text and structure' — the last two, if anything, lean pole_a, contradicting the +1). The pole_b placement is inferred from pre-bench pro bono immigration advocacy, i.e., client work, not interpretive method, and he has zero authored opinions to evaluate. This is exactly the campaign-boilerplate inference the rubric warns against; omission is the honest treatment.

review_notes: Larson judicial -1/high is well grounded (repeated restraint/depoliticization statements: no legislating from the bench, Blake 'motivated by ideology', restoring trust above partisan interests) and correctly stops short of -2 absent an originalist framework. Larson safety 0 is a genuine documented mix — bail-rule opposition and Blake criticism on one side, six years of Therapeutic Courts Committee service and 'compassion and accountability' collaboration on the other — not an unknown. Amamilo judicial 0 is right: precedent-adherence is stare decisis, not strict construction, and it offsets her access-to-justice framing. Amamilo safety +1 rests on 12-year public-defense formation plus a press-reported bail/sentencing-scrutiny record, both sanctioned bases. Miller's omitted judicial score is correct — his 'no predetermined conclusions / not in anyone's pocket' statements are boilerplate. Angelis's omitted safety score is correct (no criminal-law formation, no bail/diversion positions). None of the upheld scores relies on the WAGOP recommendation or Democratic endorsement patterns.
